research_answer: The predictive power of implied comparative advantage — derived from a country's existing export basket — improves substantially over longer time horizons. Using data from 1995 to 2016, the minimum explanatory power (R²) across any time window exceeds 8.5%, the mean across all horizons is 14.6%, and it reaches roughly 20% at the full 21-year horizon. This means that the structural gaps between where a country 'should' have comparative advantage (based on its neighbor industries and similar countries) and where it actually does are better signals of long-run export evolution than short-run shifts. The implication is that trade diversification is a slow, path-dependent process — not a sudden reorientation — and that the product space and country space together act as structural attractors pulling export patterns toward their implied equilibrium over decades.
supporting_quotes: Figure 2 shows adjusted R² values for international trade regressions (including base-year controls) over all possible year combinations. Each regression explains a sizable portion of the variation, with the lowest adjusted R² exceeding 8.5%, and a mean R² of 14.6%. Explanatory power generally improves as the interval increases
